tags.description: Historic 1892 British steamship wreck resting in 15-25 feet (5-7.6m) of water approximately 150 yards offshore from Delray Beach. Shore dive accessible from Delray Beach municipal beach. Most of the vessel is buried and deteriorated; the boiler field is the primary remaining intact feature. Site hosts abundant fish life including tarpon. Scattered boiler debris across roughly 70 feet creates an interesting exploration for beginner divers.
tags.name: S.S. Inchulva / Delray Wreck
Julia Tuttle Causeway Artificial Reef
tags.description: Artificial reef complex in Biscayne Bay consisting of 727 tons of limestone boulders deployed on April 18, 2019 by Miami-Dade County atop the previously deployed Larson Barge at approximately 25° 48.899' N, 80° 10.269' W in about 32 feet (9.8m) of water. Additional material was deployed in 2019, 2020, 2022, and 2023. Primarily a fisheries-enhancement reef; not a mainstream recreational dive operator destination.

tags.name: Lofthus
tags.description: Historic 1868 three-masted iron barque (ex-Cashmere) wrecked February 4, 1898 off Manalapan and Boynton Beach. Scattered remains in 15-20 feet (5-6m) of water. Florida's eighth Underwater Archaeological Preserve and listed on the National Register of Historic Places January 6, 2004. Shallow depth makes this accessible to beginner divers and snorkelers.

Historic 1898 cargo steamship (3,297 GRT, 325 feet) wrecked May 26, 1900 at the Pompano Dropoff reef south of Hillsboro Inlet due to navigational error. Scattered remains in 16-31 feet (5-9.5m) of water. Florida Underwater Archaeological Preserve #5, listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2001. One of Florida's most-visited underwater preserves; accessible by boat from Pompano Beach and Fort Lauderdale.
tags.name: SS Copenhagen Shipwreck

tags.description: Premier shore dive south of Anglins Fishing Pier (currently closed following hurricane damage) in Lauderdale-by-the-Sea. Three reef lines run parallel to shore; the first reef begins 100-400 feet from the Datura Avenue beach entry at 12-18 feet (3.7-5.5m). A 10-12 foot ledge runs along the reef. Regular marine life includes sea turtles, nurse sharks, stingrays, moray eels, spiny lobster, tarpon, and tropical reef fish. Caribbean reef sharks are occasionally sighted. One of the most accessible reef dives in South Florida.
tags.name: Anglins Pier Reef

tags.description: Iconic shallow-water shore dive at Phil Foster Park (900 E Blue Heron Blvd), Riviera Beach, in Lake Worth Lagoon. Depths typically 10-20 feet (3-6m), maximum approximately 21 feet. Named 2013 Best Dive Site in the World by Sport Diver magazine. High-tide slack water essential — dive within one hour of high slack. Outstanding macro destination: seahorses, pipefish, Atlantic longarm octopus, multiple frogfish species, sea robins, batfish, and flying gurnards. Phil Foster Park Snorkel Trail (installed 2012) runs approximately 800 feet with 600+ tons of Anastasia limestone boulders, reef balls, and mini wrecks. Dive flag required; stay clear of boat channel under the west bridge.

Limestone ledge reef running north-south for several miles off West Palm Beach at approximately 50-65 feet (15-20m). Named for its alignment offshore from the Breakers Hotel. A continuous ledge rising 5-10 feet off the sand is the main feature; the northern Fourth Window section is the standard charter drop point. Live-boat Gulf Stream drift dive. Charters run by Force-E Riviera Beach and Stuart Scuba.

Five decommissioned Gulf of Mexico oil platforms donated by Tenneco Oil Company and sunk October 3, 1985 off Hallandale Beach by the Broward County Artificial Reef Program. Three platforms within recreational limits have top decks at approximately 60, 80, and 100 feet (18, 24, 30m); two deeper platforms sit at roughly 185-205 feet for technical divers. Aligned east-west. Hurricane Andrew (1992) tilted the deep platforms to approximately 45 degrees. Advanced Open Water certification required.

Shallow reef and ledge system north of Miami near Haulover Inlet, typically run as a drift dive. Part of the broader Haulover Artificial Reef complex. Depths range from 20-40 feet (6-12m) along the main ledge. The nearby Deep Trench — a 1920s-era waste outfall pipe cut — drops from approximately 48 to 60 feet and provides current relief. Boat dive only; strong tidal currents — SMB essential. Snapper, grouper, hogfish, moray eels, and spiny lobster common.

Atlantis-themed underwater memorial reef approximately 3.25 miles offshore from Key Biscayne at 40 feet (12m) depth. Functions as an underwater cemetery for cremated remains and as a living reef habitat. Accessible for beginner divers. Boat dive only; mooring buoys on site. Great barracuda, southern stingrays, moray eels, and spiny lobster are regularly reported.

Part of Broward County's artificial reef program. The main feature is the Rapa Nui Reef, a 150-foot steel barge deployed June 7, 2015 approximately one mile off Deerfield Beach Pier at 67-75 feet (20-23m). The barge rolled during the controlled sinking, overturning onto the 15 concrete Moai sculptures (6-22 feet tall) created by artist Dennis MacDonald, funded by philanthropist Margaret Blume. The inverted hull rises approximately 9 feet off the sand and supports dense fish life and coral growth.

Primary beach entry for Lauderdale-by-the-Sea shore diving via Datura Avenue at El Mar Drive. First reef line 100-400 feet from shore at 12-20 feet depth. The Shipwreck Snorkel Trail (dedicated 2002 by Jean-Michel Cousteau and the Marine Archaeological Council) sits in approximately 8-10 feet of water with five concrete replica cannons, a concrete anchor, and a ballast pile. Tank rack and freshwater rinse on the beach. Metered parking via PayByPhone.
